cover.title: THE PIONEER
cover.subtitle: The one who lights the fire, and forgets to check who's moving with them.
cover.tagline: You light the fire and keep pulling forward. What you're learning now is how not to carry it alone.

profile_scores.caption: High drive and expression, low stability. You light the fire and keep pulling forward, but you don't know how to stop.

short_summary: You move fast and pull people into momentum before they even decide to follow, and you rarely stop to check if they're moving as much as you are.
The exhaustion you've felt wasn't from leading too much. It was from never once meeting someone who pulled alongside you.
What you're still learning isn't how to move first. It's how to trust that the relationship stays safe even when you're not the one pulling it forward.

full_report.headline: THE PIONEER
full_report.intro: You're not someone who agonizes before falling in love. If you're drawn to someone, you just move. You make the opening, approach first, see what happens. This report follows the pattern underneath that: why you keep becoming the one who pulls the relationship forward alone, what it's cost you, and what actually changes it.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: You do not spend long agonizing before you move toward someone. If you feel drawn to them, you usually make the opening and see what happens.
People may read that as confidence. They see someone who acts quickly and sets the tone. What they may not see is how much hope you have already placed in the possibility.
When you see potential, you want to move. That courage is a real strength. The difficulty begins when the relationship keeps moving because of your energy alone.
chapters.profile_note: High Drive · High Expression, Low Stability. You light the fire and keep pulling forward, but you don't know how to stop.

chapters.title: The Pattern You Keep Repeating
body: You may already recognize where this is going. You felt drawn to someone, so you moved. You made plans, created momentum, and suggested the next step. They came along.

Then one day, you may realize that most of the direction has been coming from you. It does not mean you did not care about them. The relationship may simply have become something that stops whenever you stop pulling.
chapters.highlight: The exhaustion was not only from leading. It came from never feeling someone move beside you with the same intention.

chart.title: THE EXHAUSTION CURVE
chart.description: You hold on through sheer drive as the relationship goes on, then suddenly stop, all at once.

chapters.title: What You've Gotten Wrong About Yourself

items.truth: You may hesitate more than people realize. You often move before the hesitation has time to show, so action starts looking like certainty.
items.truth_short: You may hesitate too. You simply move before anyone can see it.

items.belief: If I move first, they'll follow.
items.truth: What you saw in them may not have been imagined. But following your momentum and choosing the relationship for themselves are different things.
items.truth_short: Following your lead is not always the same as choosing the relationship.

items.belief: I got hurt because I approached first.
items.truth: Approaching first was not the problem. The difficulty may have been continuing without checking whether the other person wanted the same pace and direction.
items.truth_short: The issue may not be moving first, but missing the check in as you go.

items.belief: Deciding fast has never cost me anything.
items.truth: Deciding quickly can create momentum, but it can also leave little space between seeing potential and confirming what is actually there.
items.truth_short: Speed can help you begin, but it can also make confirmation come too late.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: Different people, but the shape of it can look the same. You may be drawn to people who are unsure, hesitant, or relieved when someone else sets the direction.
When someone follows your lead, it can feel like connection. But following the path you opened and choosing the relationship for themselves are not the same.
chapters.highlight: Someone may follow your momentum. A committed partner also creates movement of their own.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need is not someone who only reacts to your energy.
What may help is someone who proposes things, makes choices, and keeps the relationship moving without waiting for you to do it first.
chapters.action_sentence: In a healthier relationship, momentum can come from both sides.

chapters.title: Where You Go From Here
body: You already know how to approach someone and create movement.
What you may be learning next is how to stay connected when you are not leading every step, without turning the pause into a test or assuming the relationship will disappear.
chapters.transition_note: You've spent a long time creating momentum. The next step is learning how to share direction instead of carrying it alone.

chapters.title: The Types You Keep Falling For

items.type_title: Someone Who Has Not Decided Yet
items.description: They may be unsure of what they want, while you can already see a direction. Over time, you may realize you have been deciding what should happen for both of you.

items.type_title: Someone Who Reacts to Your Energy
items.description: They respond when you create momentum. At first that can feel like a good rhythm. But when you stop, they may stop too.
They admire how quickly you decide. But admiration for your certainty does not always mean they know how to stay close when you are unsure.
They may want some of the courage and momentum they see in you. The question is whether they begin moving too, or remain beside the path while you do all the work.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: These signs may have shown up earlier than you noticed. Taking the lead can feel so natural that the imbalance is easy to miss.

signal_strength.label: You decide almost everything. Plans, dates, what to talk about
Their agreement starts to feel like proof that both of you want the same thing
The pace of the relationship, the next step, the direction. You decide all of it
They've almost never proposed anything first

chapters.core_signal: If movement disappears whenever you pause, the relationship may be depending too heavily on your energy.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like for You

checklist.title: The relationship doesn't stop just because you're not pulling.
checklist.description: You can pause or follow sometimes, and the relationship still keeps moving.

checklist.title: They stay even when you hesitate.
checklist.description: You do not have to stay certain all the time. They can remain close when you are unsure.

checklist.title: They're drawn to you, not your energy.
checklist.description: Their interest is not limited to the energy and direction you provide.

checklist.title: Deciding together makes you less lonely, not more.
checklist.description: Shared decisions leave you feeling supported instead of responsible for everything.

chapters.title: What Happens In Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you: You're vulnerable to someone who is comfortable letting you create all the momentum. They may agree with every plan without bringing direction of their own. Over time, you can end up leading a relationship that only moves when you do.
sections.who_steadies_you: A steadier match is someone who can move with you and also begin things themselves. They do not compete for control, but they do bring choices, plans, and effort of their own.
sections.same_pattern_repeats: You may still feel drawn quickly when you see possibility. That alone will not decide how it goes. What matters is whether the other person begins moving from their own intention, not only in response to yours.
sections.next_signs: This time, you may catch it in smaller details. You suggest the plans, open every conversation, and choose the next step. Notice whether the other person gradually begins creating movement too.
sections.try_this: Before taking the next step, ask what they want and what they would choose. Leave enough room for an answer, then look for consistent initiative over time. Do not create silence as a test or use one response as the whole verdict.
sections.final_line: What matters is whether direction, effort, and momentum begin coming from both sides.

action_plan.summary: You already know how to move first. What's left is learning to wait for someone to move toward you too.
action_items: This week, ask the other person to choose one plan instead of deciding it yourself.
Before investing in someone's potential, notice whether they are already moving toward it.
When you feel the urge to fill every pause, ask what they want before creating the next step.

closing.message: You've spent years learning how to move first. This report covered why you always end up being the one who approaches first, and how you relearn the art of stepping back.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
